title: Competing-Risk Discrete Hazard Instead Of Plain Next-Lap Binary
summary: This develops the earlier hazard idea into a more testable form: discrete-time survival directly models interval hazard with censoring, and this problem has an obvious competing terminal state when a stint ends by race finish rather than a pit stop.
rationale: This develops the earlier hazard idea into a more testable form: discrete-time survival directly models interval hazard with censoring, and this problem has an obvious competing terminal state when a stint ends by race finish rather than a pit stop. That structure is more faithful than treating each row as independent binary classification.
implementation_hint: Create person-period data within each stint or driver-race trajectory, labeling each lap as `pit next interval`, `survive`, or `finish before pit`. Fit either a cause-specific hazard pair of models or a single discrete-time hazard model with time-varying covariates; use only the current-lap hazard as the submission score.
expected_effect: More temporally coherent probabilities near stint end, with better handling of rows that are close to race finish and should be low-risk despite old tyres.
risk: Larger training table and more engineering. If not carefully weighted, the expanded data can overemphasize long easy-survival stretches.
